title: Comprehensive Guide Regression Python INFS3822
body:
INFS3822 Comprehensive Guide in Python including all steps
             required to build ML Regression Models



This guide summarises all the steps you learned during weeks 3 and 4 including data loading, pre-
processing (data cleaning and transformation including the conversion of categorical variables into
dummy variables), feature selection (using Pair Plot and Correlation Matrix Heatmap), splitting
data into training and testing datasets, model training and testing to assess the performance of the
models. This guide including the steps requires for building ML models for regression problems
using linear regression, decision tree and neural network in Python.
First, upload the file data_online_retail_customers.csv into this notebook on Google Colab.
Once the dataset is uploaded, you can conduct the following steps.


1     Importing Libraries
The following libraries are the ones used during weeks 3 and 4 that cover the required libraries for
logistic regression, decision tree, and neural networks.
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from tensorflow . keras . models import Sequential
from tensorflow . keras . layers import Dense
from keras . metrics import RootMeanSquaredError
from sklearn . inspection import per mu tat io n_i mpo rt anc e
from sklearn . base import BaseEstimator , RegressorMixin
from sklearn . model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn . linear_model import LinearRegression
from sklearn . tree import DecisionTreeRegressor , export_text , plot_tree
from sklearn . metrics import mean_squared_error , mean_absolute_error , r2_score ,
    accuracy_score
from sklearn . metrics import confusion_matrix
from sklearn import preprocessing
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib . pyplot as plt
from pandas . api . types import is_numeric_dtype , is_categorical_dtype , is_bool_dtype




2     Loading the Data from a CSV File
# Load the data
data = pd . read_csv ( ' d a t a _ o n l i n e _ r e t a i l _ c u s t o m e r s . csv ')




                                                                   1
 3    Data Cleaning
# Check for missing values
missing_values = data . isna ()
print ( missing_values . any () . any () )



False



# Remove rows with missing values , if any
data = data . dropna ()




4    Data Transformation
# Check the data type of all variables
print ( data . dtypes )



Customer_ID                         int64
Age                                 int64
Gender                             object
Annual_Income                     float64
Total_Spend                       float64
Years_as_Customer                   int64
Num_of_Purchases                    int64
Num_of_Returns                      int64
Num_of_Support_Contacts             int64
Satisfaction_Score                  int64
Last_Purchase_Days_Ago              int64
Email_Opt_In                         bool
Promotion_Response                 object
Churned                              bool
dtype: object



# Convert categorical columns ( ' object ' type ) into ' category ' type
data [ ' Gender '] = data [ ' Gender ' ]. astype ( ' category ')
data [ ' Pr omotion_Response '] = data [ ' Promotion_Response ' ]. astype ( ' category ')



Further data transformations
Uncomment the lines you need by removing the # Modify the code to fit your needs

                                                 2
 # In this dataset , the target variable is Churned , and it is of type ' bool ' ( i . e .
    boolean ) .
# If the target variable is binary ( e . g . Yes / No , Good / Bad , Success / Fail ) , make
    sure you convert it into boolean variable as follows :
# data [ ' Churned '] = data [ ' Churned '] == ' Yes '

#   Convert date columns into datetime based on your objectives .
#   For further details , refer to W e e k 4 _ P r e _ S e m i n a r _ U s i n g _ P y t h o n _ f o r _ C l u s t e r i n g
#   data [ ' vdate '] = pd . to_datetime ( data [ ' vdate ']) # for dates in the format m / d / yyyy
#   data [ ' vdate '] = pd . to_datetime ( data [ ' vdate '] , dayfirst = True ) # for dates in the
      format d / m / yyyy

# If you have a variable representing a date , you can transform it into a
    categorical variable ( Think of what would be the categories that are relevant
    to your analysis objectives )
# For further details , refer to W e e k 4 _ P r e _ S e m i n a r _ U s i n g _ P y t h o n _ f o r _ C l u s t e r i n g
# Create a categorical variable for seasons
# def get_season ( date ) :
#     month = date . month
#     if month in [12 , 1 , 2]:
#           return ' Winter '
#     elif month in [3 , 4 , 5]:
#           return ' Spring '
#     elif month in [6 , 7 , 8]:
#           return ' Summer '
#     else :
#           return ' Autumn '
#
# data [ ' season '] = data [ ' vdate ']. apply ( get_season )
# data [ ' season '] = data [ ' season ']. astype ( ' category ')

# You can transform some data values . For example , convert '5+ ' to numeric 5 in
    the ' rcount ' column ( W e e k 4 _ P r e _ S e m i n a r _ U s i n g _ P y t h o n _ f o r _ C l u s t e r i n g )
# data [ ' rcount '] = data [ ' rcount ']. replace ( '5+ ' , 5) . astype ( int )



# Scale the numeric input data
# Identify numeric columns
numeric_columns = data . select_dtypes ( include =[ ' float64 ' , ' int64 ' ]) . columns

# Scale numeric columns
scaler = preprocessing . StandardScaler () . fit ( data [ numeric_columns ])
data [ numeric_columns ] = scaler . transform ( data [ numeric_columns ])




5      Feature Selection
# Split the data into inputs ( X as features ) and outputs ( y as target variable ) .
# Initial feature selection ( i . e . selecting which variables should be included in
    X ) is required here as follows .
# You can exclude variables that represent unique identifiers because they don 't
    have any impact on the target variable .
# You can exclude variables that are text descriptions and not useful as
    categories .
# You can exclude categorical variables that have too many possible values , as
    these would generate too many dummy variables . A model with too many variables


                                                              3
     cannot perform well and sometimes cause the machine to run out of memory . This
    would also help you focus on the most important variables .
X = data . drop ( columns =[ ' Customer_ID ' , ' Total_Spend ' ])
y = data [ " Total_Spend " ]




Pair Plot and Correlation Matrix Heatmap
# Determine the type of the target variable
targe t_is_numeric = is_numeric_dtype ( y ) and not is_bool_dtype ( y )
t a r g e t _ i s _cate gorica l = is_categorical_dtype ( y )
targe t_is_boolean = is_bool_dtype ( y )

target = " Total_Spend "

# Plot each feature against the target variable using appropriate plots in a grid
num_features = len ( X . columns )
num_cols = 3
num_rows = ( num_features + num_cols - 1) // num_cols # Ceiling division

fig , axes = plt . subplots ( num_rows , num_cols , figsize =(15 , num_rows * 5) )

# Plot each feature against the target variable
for i , feature in enumerate ( X . columns ) :
    row = i // num_cols
    col = i % num_cols
    ax = axes [ row , col ]
    # Determine the type of the feature
    fe at ure_is_numeric = is_numeric_dtype ( X [ feature ]) and not is_bool_dtype ( X [
    feature ])
    fe at ure_is_boolean = is_bool_dtype ( X [ feature ])
    f e a t u re _i s_c ate go ric al = is_categorical_dtype ( X [ feature ])

    # Select appropriate plot
     if ( t ar get_is _categ orica l or target_is_boolean ) and feature_is_numeric :
             sns . boxplot ( x =y , y = X [ feature ] , ax = ax )
             ax . set_title ( f ' Box Plot of { feature } by { target } ')
     elif target_is_numeric and feature_is_numeric :
             sns . scatterplot ( x = X [ feature ] , y =y , ax = ax )
             ax . set_title ( f ' Scatter Plot of { feature } vs { target } ')
     elif ( targ et_is_ categ orical or target_is_boolean ) and ( feature_is_boolean or
    f e a t u r e _is _c ate go ric al ) :
             sns . countplot ( x = X [ feature ] , hue =y , ax = ax )
             ax . set_title ( f ' Count Plot of { feature } by { target } ')
     elif target_is_numeric and ( feature_is_boolean or fea tur e_ is_ ca teg ori ca l ) :
             sns . boxplot ( x = X [ feature ] , y =y , ax = ax )
             ax . set_title ( f ' Box Plot of { target } by { feature } ')

# Remove any empty subplots
for i in range ( num_features , num_rows * num_cols ) :
    fig . delaxes ( axes . flatten () [ i ])

# Adjust layout
plt . tight_layout ()
plt . show ()




                                              4
 5
 # Convert categorical variables into dummy variables to include individual
    categories as variables . Please make sure that you do this before constructing
    the correlation heatmap . This would help you identify the most important
    features to be included in your models . It is also important to do this before
    training the models .
X = pd . get_dummies (X , drop_first = True )



# Let 's plot the correlation matrix heatmap to check which variables are important
     ( i . e . correlated with the target variable ) .

import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib . pyplot as plt

# Compute the correlation matrix
corr_matrix = pd . concat ([ X , y ] , axis =1) . corr ()

# Plot the heatmap
plt . figure ( figsize =(12 , 8) )
sns . heatmap ( corr_matrix , annot = True , fmt = ' .2 f ' , cmap = ' coolwarm ')
plt . title ( ' Correlation Matrix Heatmap ')
plt . show ()




                                                    6
 # Further feature selection based on the results of pair plots and / or correlation
    matrix heatmap .
# You can exclude variables that are not correlated with the targe variable .
# When 2 variables ( other than the target variable ) are highly correlated to each
    other , you can remove one of them to address multi - colinearity problem ( keep
    the one that is highly correlated with the target variable ) .
X = X . drop ( columns =[ ' Satisfaction_Score ' , ' P r o m o t i o n _ R e s p o n s e _ R e s p o n d e d ' ])




6    Split the data into training and testing sets
# Split the data into training and testing sets
X_train , X_test , y_train , y_test = train_test_split (X , y , test_size =0.2)




7    Train and test a Linear Regression Model
# Train a linear regression model on the training data
model = LinearRegression ()
model . fit ( X_train , y_train )

# Make predictions on the testing data
predictions = model . predict ( X_test )

# Calculate Mean Absolute Error ( MAE )
mae = m e a n_absolute_error ( y_test , predictions )

# Calculate Root Mean Squared Error ( RMSE )
rmse = np . sqrt ( mean_squared_error ( y_test , predictions ) )

# Calculate R - squared
r_squared = model . score ( X_test , y_test )

# Print evaluation metrics
print ( f " Mean Absolute Error ( MAE ) : { mae } " )
print ( f " Root Mean Squared Error ( RMSE ) : { rmse } " )
print ( f "R - squared : { r_squared } " )

# Plot actual vs predicted values
plt . scatter ( y_test , predictions )
plt . xlabel ( " Actual Total_Spend " )
plt . ylabel ( " Predicted Total_Spend " )
plt . title ( " Actual vs Predicted Total_Spend " )
plt . plot ([ y_test . min () , y_test . max () ] , [ y_test . min () , y_test . max () ] , 'k - - ' , lw =2)
plt . show ()

# Display feature coefficients
coefficients = pd . DataFrame ({
    ' Feature ': X . columns ,
    ' Coefficient ': model . coef_
})



                                                         7
 print ( coefficients )

8    Train a Decision Tree Regression Model
# Train a decision tree model on the training data
# You can also adjust max_depth to decide the depth of the tree .
model = D e cision TreeR egress or ( max_depth =4)
model . fit ( X_train , y_train )

# Make predictions on the testing data
y_pred = model . predict ( X_test )

# Make predictions on the testing data
y_pred = model . predict ( X_test )

# Calculate regression metrics
# Calculate Mean Squared Error ( MSE )
mse = m ea n_squared_error ( y_test , y_pred )
# Calculate Mean Absolute Error ( MAE )
mae = m e a n_absolute_error ( y_test , y_pred )
# Calculate R - squared
r2 = r2_score ( y_test , y_pred )

print ( f " Mean Squared Error : { mse } " )
print ( f " Mean Absolute Error : { mae } " )
print ( f "R - squared : { r2 } " )

# Plot the decision tree
plt . figure ( figsize =(20 ,10) )
plot_tree ( model , filled = True , feature_names = X_train . columns )
plt . title ( " Decision Tree Regression Model " )
plt . show ()

# Display the decision tree structure in text form
tree_structure = export_text ( model , feature_names = list ( X_train . columns ) )
print ( tree_structure )

# Display the feature importance scores
f ea t u re _ importances = model . feature_importances_
features = X_train . columns
impor tance_scores = dict ( zip ( features , feature_importances ) )

print ( " Feature Importance Scores : " )
for feature , score in importance_scores . items () :
    print ( f " { feature }: { score } " )

9    Train a Neural Network Model
# Please ensure that all variables are converted into the float32 data type , as
    TensorFlow and Keras neural networks expect numeric values in float32 format as
     input .
X_train = X_train . astype ( ' float32 ')
y_train = y_train . astype ( ' float32 ')
X_test = X_test . astype ( ' float32 ')
y_test = y_test . astype ( ' float32 ')




                                                11
 # Define the model
model = Sequential ()
model . add ( Dense (12 , input_shape =( len ( X_train . columns ) ,) , activation = ' relu ') )
model . add ( Dense (6 , activation = ' relu ') )
model . add ( Dense (1 , activation = ' linear ') ) # Change to ' linear ' for regression

# Compile the model
model . compile ( loss = ' mean_squared_error ' , optimizer = ' adam ' , metrics =[
    R o o t M e anSquaredError () ])

# Train the model
history = model . fit ( X_train , y_train , epochs =5 , batch_size =200 , validation_split
    =0.2)

# Evaluate the model
loss , rmse = model . evaluate ( X_test , y_test )
print ( f ' Root Mean Squared Error : { rmse } ')

# Make predictions with the model
predictions = model . predict ( X_test )

# Calculate additional regression metrics
mse = m ea n_squared_error ( y_test , predictions )
mae = m e a n_absolute_error ( y_test , predictions )
r2 = r2_score ( y_test , predictions )

print ( f " Mean Squared Error : { mse } " )
print ( f " Mean Absolute Error : { mae } " )
print ( f "R - squared : { r2 } " )

# Plot training & validation loss values
plt . figure ( figsize =(10 , 5) )
plt . plot ( history . history [ ' loss ' ])
plt . plot ( history . history [ ' val_loss ' ])
plt . title ( ' Model loss ')
plt . xlabel ( ' Epoch ')
plt . ylabel ( ' Loss ')
plt . legend ([ ' Train ' , ' Validation '] , loc = ' upper right ')
plt . show ()

# Plot predictions vs true values
plt . figure ( figsize =(10 , 5) )
plt . scatter ( y_test , predictions , alpha =0.5)
plt . plot ([ min ( y_test ) , max ( y_test ) ] , [ min ( y_test ) , max ( y_test ) ] , color = ' red ')   #
     Diagonal line
plt . title ( ' Predictions vs True Values ')
plt . xlabel ( ' True Values ')
plt . ylabel ( ' Predictions ')
plt . show ()

# Display feature importance scores
# Wrapper for Keras model
class K e r asRe gress orWrap per ( BaseEstimator , RegressorMixin ) :
    def __init__ ( self , model ) :
          self . model = model

     def fit ( self , X , y ) :
         self . model . fit (X , y , epochs =1 , batch_size =50 , verbose =0)
         return self


                                                      12
     def predict ( self , X ) :
        return self . model . predict ( X )

    def score ( self , X , y ) :
        predictions = self . model . predict ( X )
        return - mean_squared_error (y , predictions )

# Create an instance of the wrapper
wrapped_model = Keras Regre ssorWr apper ( model )

# Calculate permutation importance
results = pe rmu ta tio n_i mp ort an ce ( wrapped_model , X_test , y_test , scoring = '
    n e g _ m e a n_s qu are d_ err or ' , n_repeats =3 , random_state =42)

# Summarize feature importance
for i in range ( X_test . shape [1]) :
    print ( f ' Feature : { X_test . columns [ i ]} , Importance : { results . importances_mean [ i
    ]:.4 f } ')
